solution: The exponential term \( [[base]]^{n} \) grows faster than any power of \( n \), so it dominates both the numerator and denominator. The limit is the ratio of the exponential coefficients: \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the dominant (exponential) coefficients.
The polynomial terms are dominated by the exponential, so the limit is the coefficient ratio, not \( 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

solution: Factorial growth eventually outpaces any fixed exponential base: for a fixed \( [[base]] \), the ratio of consecutive terms \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} = \dfrac{[[base]]}{n+1} \to 0 \), so \( a_n \to 0 \). Thus \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the constant multiplier \( [[c]] \), ignoring that \( n! \) grows without bound.
This is just the exponential base \( [[base]] \), ignoring that \( n! \) grows without bound.
This wrongly assumes the exponential in the numerator dominates; factorial growth dominates any fixed exponential.
This multiplies the two given constants instead of comparing growth rates.
This wrongly assumes the two growth rates balance to a finite nonzero ratio.
This divides the two given constants instead of comparing growth rates.

solution: The factorial term \( n! \) grows faster than any fixed exponential, so it dominates BOTH the numerator and the denominator; the exponential terms become negligible in comparison. Hence \( a_n \to \dfrac{n!}{n!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats the exponential coefficients as dominant, as if \( n! \) were absent.
This wrongly assumes the numerator vanishes relative to the denominator; both are dominated by the same \( n! \) term.
This wrongly compares the exponential bases, which are dominated by \( n! \) and so don't affect the limit.
This multiplies the exponential coefficient and base instead of recognizing both are negligible next to \( n! \).
This guesses a common 'nice' limit value without comparing growth rates.
This guesses a common 'nice' limit value without comparing growth rates.

solution: Factor out the larger base: \( a_n = [[p]]\left(1 + \left(\tfrac{[[q]]}{[[p]]}\right)^{n}\right)^{1/n} \). Since \( [[q]] < [[p]] \), \( \left(\tfrac{[[q]]}{[[p]]}\right)^{n} \to 0 \), and the whole factor \( \left(1+\cdots\right)^{1/n}\to 1 \). So \( a_n \to [[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This picks the smaller base instead of the larger (dominant) one.
This adds the two bases instead of taking the dominant one under the \( n \)th root.
This averages the two bases instead of taking the dominant one under the \( n \)th root.
This takes the geometric mean of the two bases instead of the dominant one under the \( n \)th root.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Any power \( n^{[[j]]} \) with \( [[j]] \ge 1 \) grows faster than \( \ln(n) \), so the denominator dominates the numerator. Hence \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the logarithm and the polynomial as comparable growth rates and takes a leading-coefficient ratio; the polynomial actually dominates.
This is just the logarithm's coefficient, ignoring that the denominator grows without bound.
This wrongly assumes the logarithm dominates; polynomial growth always beats logarithmic growth.
This takes a ratio of the constant terms instead of comparing the growth rates of the leading terms.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
